title: Navier-Stokes Equations at Critical Regularity
speaker: Stan Palasek
abstract: This talk is concerned with solutions of the 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes equations that are bounded in a critical space. From small initial data, these solutions are known to be globally well-posed due to classical work of Fujita-Kato and others. I will discuss the problem of large data, including recent constructions of pathological behavior such as non-uniqueness, norm growth, and Type I blow-up. This is joint work with A. Cheskidov, M. Coiculescu, and M. Dai.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Analysis and Mathematical Physics

proof_stages.label: Critical-regularity landscape for Navier–Stokes
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the incompressible Navier–Stokes Cauchy problem, classifies data spaces by scaling as subcritical, critical, or supercritical, reviews the known well-posedness/ill-posedness picture, and isolates large-data questions in borderline critical spaces such as BMO^{-1}.

proof_stages.label: Dyadic self-similarity as a nonuniqueness mechanism
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing convex integration and instability-based nonuniqueness frameworks, the speaker introduces an Obukhov-type dyadic shell model whose discrete scaling symmetry and alternating even/odd self-similarity suggest a route to sharp nonuniqueness.

proof_stages.label: Partial PDE embedding and BMO^{-1} nonuniqueness theorem
proof_stages.summary: The dyadic mechanism is translated into Bony-type Navier–Stokes interactions, where the high-high-to-low inverse cascade can be embedded but the upward cascade cannot, leading to a theorem giving two distinct global Koch–Tataru-class solutions from the same large BMO^{-1} initial datum.

proof_stages.label: Alternating heat-dominated and inverse-cascade approximate flows
proof_stages.summary: The initial datum is decomposed over lacunary frequency shells so that each shell admits two compatible evolutions—one essentially heat-flow decay and one rapidly damped by inverse cascade from the shell above—and alternating these choices on even and odd shells produces two distinct approximate solutions with the same datum.

proof_stages.label: Oscillatory building blocks and critical amplitude recursion
proof_stages.summary: Frequency-localized oscillatory shear-type pieces are designed so the inverse-cascade equation reduces to an integrable ODE, and a Nash-style decomposition of positive definite tensors supplies enough directions to prescribe coefficients whose recursion lands exactly at critical size.

proof_stages.label: Subcritical error correction by a semigroup fixed point
proof_stages.summary: The approximate solutions solve forced Navier–Stokes with a source term that is not merely small but subcritical, and a zero-initial-data correction is constructed using estimates for the linearized semigroup around the critical background together with a fixed point argument.

proof_stages.label: Finite inverse cascade destroying BMO^{-1} a priori bounds
proof_stages.summary: The talk turns from nonuniqueness to quantitative a priori estimates, showing that a single high-frequency concentration can cascade energy downward so that BMO^{-1}-bounded smooth data lead to arbitrarily large critical-scale solutions, unlike compactness results in better critical spaces.

proof_stages.label: Infinite cascade and type-I weak-strong nonuniqueness
proof_stages.summary: Letting the finite cascade originate from frequency infinity yields weak solutions from any smooth datum that are smooth for positive time, obey and saturate type-I bounds near a prescribed time, remain bounded in a slightly better-than-BMO^{-1} critical norm, and violate weak-strong uniqueness while nearly saturating logarithmic regularity criteria.

examples.name: Good critical spaces L^3 and H^{1/2}
examples.note: These spaces make scaling-critical data concrete and illustrate the classical Fujita–Kato/Kato local and small-data theory before the talk moves to rougher critical spaces.
examples.engagement_reason: L^3 and H^{1/2} are cited as standard good critical spaces with known well-posedness facts, but no calculation or construction is carried out specifically in them

examples.name: Bourgain–Pavlović norm inflation
examples.note: This previously known small-data ill-posedness mechanism in very rough critical Besov spaces is used as a contrast to the speaker’s single-frequency cascade mechanism for BMO^{-1}-type data.
examples.engagement_reason: the Bourgain–Pavlović norm-inflation mechanism is contrasted in detail by tracing how many small frequency components add low-frequency contributions and why this fails in BMO^{-1}

examples.name: Lorenz-type butterfly-effect model
examples.note: Prompted by a question about passive scalars, the speaker compares the infinite-cascade phenomenon to Lorenz’s model where high-frequency perturbations interacting with a background field can create a butterfly-effect form of nonuniqueness.
examples.engagement_reason: the Lorenz butterfly-effect model is briefly described as a related high-frequency perturbation scenario, without any concrete analysis of the model
